turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the court in Case 23677 Royal Canin v. Wullschleger.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This lawsuit is about whether a pet food company violated the law in the way it marketed it's dog food. But our ruling today has nothing to do with that substantive question, instead we address a jurisdictional matter. Can this case be decided in Federal Court or must the Federal Court kick the case to a State Court for resolution. Here is what happened in this lawsuit and how the jurisdictional issue arose. Anastasia Wullschleger purchased prescription dog food manufactured by the Royal Canin company. Because the food was only available with a veterinarian's prescription, Ms. Wullschleger assumes that it contains some medicine, but she says in her complaint that wasn't so. The dog food was ordinary dog food. The company sold it as a prescription-only product not because that was really needed but just to fool customers into paying a jacked-up price. Ms. Wullschleger was fooled and she did not like it. She asserted claims against Royal Canin under both Federal Law and State Law. Specifically, the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act and Missouri Consumer Protection Law. She brought that suit as was her choice in Missouri State Court. But Royal Canin wanted the suit to be resolved in a Federal Court so the company removed the suit there. At the time, that was completely within its rights. A defendant can remove a case from State to Federal Court if the case could have been brought in Federal Court initially. And Wullschleger's suit could have been brought in Federal Court initially. Her claims based on the Food and Drug Act fell within the Federal Court's jurisdiction over federal questions. And her claims based on State Consumer Protection Law could also be brought in Federal Court because they were factually related to her Federal Law claims. And when that's so, a Federal Court has what's called supplemental jurisdiction over the related State claims. So now the entire suit, Federal and State claims alike, was properly in Federal Court. But Ms. Wullschleger, as I told you, didn't want her suit to be decided in Federal Court. So, here's what she did. She amended her complaints to take out all the federal claims leaving only State Law claims behind, and then she went to the Federal Court and said, it no longer had jurisdiction over the suit. The Federal Claims she said were gone and the remaining State claims she said were now supplemental to nothing. As amended, this was an ordinary State Law suit, which could not have originally been brought in Federal Court. So, she asked the Federal Court to remand the suit to the State Court where it started. Royal Canin objected. It said that once the case had been properly removed, Wullschleger's amendment could not divest to Federal Court of jurisdiction. The court below agreed with Wullschleger and ordered a remand to State Court and that's the question we take up. If in a case removed to the Federal Court, a plaintiff amends her complaint to strip out all the federal claims leaving nothing but State claims behind may a Federal Court still hear the suit or does it have to remand the case to State Court. We hold today that the Federal Court must remand the case. When a plaintiff amends her complaint, the new complaint supercedes the old one, and in a situation like this, the new complaint offers no basis for a Federal Court to decide the case.Because the plaintiff has deleted all her Federal Claims, a Federal Court doesn't have ordinary federal question jurisdiction. And with any Federal anchor gone, supplemental jurisdiction over the remaining State Law claims disappears as well. The Federal Court would not have originally had jurisdiction over the all State lawsuit and the fact that there were once Federal claims in the suit which enabled removal doesn't make a difference. As amended, the suit concerns only State Law so it belongs in a State Court. In our opinion, today we show how that result is the one Congress required in a statute conferring supplemental jurisdiction on Federal Courts. We also show how that result is consistent with many other rules hinging jurisdiction on an amended complaint rather than an initial one. And finally, we reject Royal Canin's argument that our precedents demand a different outcome. Ms. Wullschleger can now bring her suit in State Court as she always wanted to. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
